verdict_reasoning: VEGFA (vascular endothelial growth factor A) is a secreted glycoprotein that binds cell-surface VEGF receptors (VEGFR1/2) in a reversible, equilibrium-dependent manner. It has no transmembrane domain, no GPI anchor, and no outer-leaflet lipidation of its own. The dominant isoforms (VEGF165, VEGF121, etc.) are constitutively secreted into the extracellular space. Some heparin-binding isoforms (VEGF189, VEGF206) bind heparan-sulfate proteoglycans on the ECM/cell surface, but this is reversible non-covalent binding in equilibrium with the soluble pool — not wash-resistant tethering to a TM partner. No stable covalent or wash-resistant post-translational anchoring to a TM scaffold has been documented. Anti-VEGFA therapeutics (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept) target the circulating/soluble pool. Ruling out contextual buckets: no documented cancer/cell-death outer-leaflet ecto-pool; no germline-restricted TM display; no lysosomal-exocytosis mechanism; no documented minority PM cycling pool; no covalent TM-partner tethering.
